mappings.rationaleAI: Heat engines converting fuel combustion into mechanical energy, including internal combustion engines (piston, turbine) and external combustion engines (steam, Stirling), address devices for utilizing chemical energy (B.8.a) and steam engines and steam power plants (B.4.a).

Reciprocating engines using Otto and diesel cycles, gas turbines, and jet engines are internal-combustion engines including gasoline, gas turbine, diesel, jet, turbojet, fan-jet, and turboprop engines (B.8.a).

Converts electrical energy into mechanical energy via magnetic field and current-carrying windings, encompassing induction motors, synchronous motors, and commutator motors utilizing AC and DC (B.5.b).

Electrochemical cells converting chemical energy into electrical energy via redox reactions, including primary and secondary (rechargeable) batteries, are batteries and fuel cells for energy conversion (C.4).

Semiconductor materials with conductivity modifiable by doping, forming p-n junctions as the basis of diodes, transistors, and integrated circuits, are solid-state devices for energy concentration and control (D.2).

Design and operation of devices using transistors, diodes, and integrated circuits for signal manipulation and switching directly addresses solid-state devices for energy concentration and control (D.2).

Design of the AC induction motor and polyphase AC power system addresses electric motors including induction and synchronous motors (B.5.b). Development of electrical power distribution infrastructure connects to devices for electric power generation (C.7).

The earliest known chemical explosive (low explosive, deflagrating propellant) composed of potassium nitrate, charcoal, and sulfur is black powder — the first chemical explosive listed under chemical explosives (B.8.b).

External combustion heat engine using steam pressure to drive a piston or turbine, analyzed via the Rankine cycle, directly addresses steam engines and steam power plants (B.4.a). Steam turbines that displaced reciprocating engines in power generation address steam turbines (B.4.b).

Electric charge, current, and electromagnetic induction broadly address devices for utilizing magnetic and electrical energy (B.5), including magnets and electromagnets (B.5.a). Power generation and transmission connect to devices for electric power generation including generators and dynamos (C.7).

Petroleum as a non-renewable hydrocarbon fuel is a nonrenewable energy source alongside coal and natural gas (A.3). Refined into gasoline, diesel, kerosene, and jet fuel, it powers internal-combustion engines (B.8.a).

Nuclear fission of uranium/plutonium and experimental fusion reactions are primary energy sources including thermonuclear reaction and nuclear fission (A.1). Civilian fission reactors supplying ~9% of global electricity are devices and materials for utilizing nuclear energy (B.9).

Gravitational potential and kinetic energy of flowing water is a recurring energy source (A.2). Watermills, waterwheels, and water turbines are devices for utilizing water energy (B.2). Hydroelectric generation uses hydraulic-turbine-driven generators for electric power generation (C.7).

Electrically conductive ionized gas governed by magnetohydrodynamics, with applications in nuclear fusion reactors and space propulsion, connects to magnetohydrodynamic power generators for energy conversion (C.3).

Silicon in solar cells addresses devices for utilizing solar energy (B.10). Silicon as the dominant semiconductor for transistors and integrated circuits addresses solid-state devices for energy concentration and control (D.2).
Incandescent, gas-discharge (fluorescent), and LED devices converting electrical energy into visible light are lamps and other lighting devices for energy conversion (C.5).

Self-propelled vehicles generating thrust via high-speed exhaust with liquid bipropellants, solid fuels, and hybrid systems are rocket engines — internal-combustion engines utilizing chemical energy (B.8.a).

Semiconductor lasers producing coherent monochromatic light through stimulated emission are optoelectronic devices for energy concentration and control (D.3), alongside optical fibres used in laser-based fiber-optic communications.

High and low explosives releasing stored chemical energy suddenly, classified by detonation velocity and sensitivity, directly address chemical explosives including nitroglycerin, dynamites, and military explosives (B.8.b).
Natural gas composed primarily of methane, extracted from geological formations and used for power generation and heating, is a nonrenewable energy source (A.3).

Solar radiation is a recurring energy source (A.2). Photovoltaic systems and concentrated solar power are devices for utilizing solar energy (B.10). Photovoltaic devices are also devices for electric power generation (C.7).

Mechanical vapour-compression cycles, thermoelectric (Peltier) effects, and heat transfer to higher-temperature sinks are devices for utilizing heat energy including heat exchangers and refrigeration equipment (B.7).

Energy as a conserved physical quantity manifesting in kinetic, potential, chemical, thermal, radiant, and nuclear forms provides the foundational framework for major types of energy useful to humankind (A).
Coal, petroleum, and natural gas as carbon-rich non-renewable energy sources supplying 77% of global primary energy are nonrenewable energy sources (A.3).
Spontaneous nuclear transformation of unstable isotopes releasing energy as radiation — alpha, beta, and gamma decay — is radioactivity as a primary energy source (A.1).

Wind as movement of air driven by differential solar heating is a recurring energy source (A.2). Wind's role in sailing and power generation connects to devices for utilizing wind energy including sails, windmills, and wind turbines (B.2).
Combustible sedimentary rock supplying roughly a quarter of global primary energy is coal as a nonrenewable energy source (A.3).

The six classical mechanical devices — lever, pulley, inclined plane, wheel and axle, wedge, screw — that redirect or amplify force using mechanical advantage address devices for utilizing muscle energy including pulley, lever, and block and tackle (B.1). Counterweight mechanisms using mechanical advantage relate to devices for utilizing gravitational energy (B.3).

Barriers impeding water flow to create reservoirs for hydroelectric power generation involve water turbines as devices for utilizing water energy (B.2). Hydroelectric power generated by hydraulic-turbine-driven generators addresses devices for electric power generation (C.7).
Electrons in cathode ray tubes and electron microscopes, with tunable carrier density in semiconductors, connect to electron tubes as devices for energy concentration and control (D.1).
